target.title: fix(subagents): preserve requester wake on visible delivery failure
target.body:
## Summary
- split requester wake success from visible message-tool delivery status for subagent completions
- treat requester-agent handoff as delivered when it runs but fails to produce required message-tool output, while recording `visibleDeliveryStatus: failed`
- persist visible completion delivery failure details on subagent run records without leaving the terminal result in pending final delivery retry state

## Why
For group/channel subagent completions, visible replies must still go through the message tool. When that visible delivery fails, the requester/parent wake should remain visible to orchestration instead of becoming a passive `pendingFinalDelivery` dead letter. This keeps the safety policy intact while preserving parent continuation.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior addressed:** subagent completion delivery where the requester/parent handoff runs but the required group/channel visible reply does not produce message-tool evidence.

**Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw source checkout on this PR branch, using the patched subagent announce delivery and registry lifecycle modules with a redacted runtime proof harness. The harness used an active requester session shape, a group/channel route configured as `message_tool_only`, and copied live terminal output from the run.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran `node --import tsx <redacted-temp-proof-script>` from the PR checkout after applying commit `9a7bbc68d3`.

**Evidence after fix:** copied terminal output from the redacted local runtime proof:

```json
{
  "proof": "pr-85308 requester wake survives visible delivery failure",
  "scenario": {
    "requester": "active parent session",
    "visibleRoute": "group/channel completion configured as message_tool_only",
    "inducedVisibleFailure": "announce agent returned no message-tool delivery evidence",
    "expectedOutcome": "requester wake is credited as delivered while visible delivery failure metadata remains recorded"
  },
  "deliveryResults": [
    {
      "delivered": true,
      "path": "direct",
      "requesterWakeStatus": "delivered",
      "visibleDeliveryRequired": true,
      "visibleDeliveryStatus": "failed",
      "visibleDeliveryError": "completion agent did not deliver through the message tool",
      "error": "completion agent did not deliver through the message tool",
      "phases": [
        {
          "phase": "direct-primary",
          "delivered": true,
          "path": "direct",
          "error": "completion agent did not deliver through the message tool",
          "requesterWakeStatus": "delivered",
          "visibleDeliveryRequired": true,
          "visibleDeliveryStatus": "failed",
          "visibleDeliveryError": "completion agent did not deliver through the message tool"
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "keyAssertions": {
    "requesterWakeDeliveredAt": true,
    "visibleCompletionDeliveryRequired": true,
    "visibleCompletionDeliveryStatus": "failed",
    "visibleCompletionDeliveryError": "completion agent did not deliver through the message tool",
    "completionAnnouncedAt": true,
    "completionDeliveredAt": true,
    "pendingFinalDeliveryCleared": true,
    "cleanupCompleted": true,
    "noUnexpectedResume": true,
    "noFallbackSendMessage": true
  },
  "finalRunRecordFields": {
    "completionDeliveredAt": 1779447271405,
    "completionAnnouncedAt": 1779447271405,
    "requesterWakeDeliveredAt": 1779447271405,
    "visibleCompletionDeliveryRequired": true,
    "visibleCompletionDeliveryStatus": "failed",
    "visibleCompletionDeliveryError": "completion agent did not deliver through the message tool",
    "lastAnnounceDeliveryError": "completion agent did not deliver through the message tool",
    "pendingFinalDelivery": null,
    "cleanupCompletedAt": 1779447271405
  }
}
```

The proof also captured the requester handoff call using `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"` with redacted session keys and no fallback `sendMessage` call.

**Observed result after fix:** requester-agent handoff returned `delivered: true` and recorded `requesterWakeStatus: "delivered"` while visible completion delivery stayed explicitly recorded as `visibleCompletionDeliveryStatus: "failed"` with `visibleCompletionDeliveryError: "completion agent did not deliver through the message tool"`. The final run record had `completionDeliveredAt`, `completionAnnouncedAt`, and `requesterWakeDeliveredAt` set; `pendingFinalDelivery` was cleared; cleanup completed; and no fallback direct `sendMessage` path ran.

**What was not tested:** this proof did not send a live Slack/Discord message to an external channel. It intentionally forced the same required-message-tool no-evidence failure path in a redacted local runtime proof so the PR can show requester wake and persisted failure metadata without posting to a real workspace.

## Tests
- `CI=true corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts -t 'preserves requester wake|records visible delivery|records requester wake separately'`
- `corepack pnpm format:check src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.ts src/agents/subagent-registry.types.ts src/agents/subagent-registry-run-manager.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core:test`

## Notes
- A broader full-file Vitest run reached 167/168 passing but timed out in an existing Telegram failure-path test that waits out the announce retry timeout.
- Plain `tsc --noEmit` exhausted Node heap on this checkout; `tsgo` checks passed instead.

target.title: fix(cli): show gateway probe duration in text output
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: gateway deep text status did not show RPC probe duration.
- Why it matters: operators need probe latency visibility during troubleshooting.
- What changed: capture RPC probe duration, print `RPC duration: <n>ms` in success/failure text output, and add tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): JSON output contract remains stable.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor

target.title: feat(cli): add script tagline mode with JS file support
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The CLI banner tagline is static (random or default); there is no way for users to supply a custom tagline from external logic (e.g. fortune, cowsay, a dynamic API call).
- **Why it matters:** Power users running self-hosted gateways want to personalise or brand the banner tagline without forking the codebase.
- **What changed:** Added a new `script` tagline mode. When `cli.banner.taglineMode` is set to `script` and `cli.banner.taglineScriptFile` points to a JS file, the banner calls that file's default export (string, sync function, or async function) to resolve the tagline at startup.  Also fixes a bug where `resolveTaglineScript` called `loadConfig()` without importing it, silently returning `undefined` for every user who set `taglineMode: "script"`.
- **What did NOT change:** All existing modes (`random`, `default`, `off`) are unaffected. Banner rendering, TTY detection, and `--json`/`--version` suppression logic are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related to #41147 (Apologies for the errors due to my unfamiliarity with branch operations.)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config key `cli.banner.taglineMode: "script"` (opt-in; existing behaviour unchanged when unset).
- New config key `cli.banner.taglineScriptFile: "<path>"` — absolute or `~`-prefixed path to a JS file whose default export provides the tagline.
- The script's default export may be a `string`, a sync `() => string`, or an `async () => Promise<string>`.
- If the script throws or returns a non-string, the tagline silently falls back to empty (same as `off`).

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **Yes** — users can execute arbitrary JS at CLI startup via `taglineScriptFile`. This is an explicit, opt-in, user-configured path; the file must be writable by the local user to set it, and the feature only runs code the user intentionally pointed at.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No** (script may make network calls, but that is the user's code, not ours)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**
- Risk: a malicious tagline script could exfiltrate data or corrupt state at CLI startup. Mitigation: the path is set explicitly in the user's own config file; no remote or plugin can inject it.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15
- Runtime/container: Bun 1.x / Node 22
- Model/provider: n/a
- Integration/channel (if any): n/a
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```yaml
  cli:
    banner:
      taglineMode: script
      taglineScriptFile: /tmp/tagline-cowsay.js
  ```

### Steps

1. Create `/tmp/tagline-cowsay.js`:
   ```js
   import { execFileSync } from "node:child_process";
   export default function () {
     return execFileSync("cowsay", ["The lobster in your shell."], { encoding: "utf8" }).trim();
   }
   ```
2. Set config: `openclaw config set cli.banner.taglineMode script && openclaw config set cli.banner.taglineScriptFile /tmp/tagline-cowsay.js`
3. Run any CLI command: `openclaw help`

### Expected

```
🦞 OpenClaw 2026.3.14 (d536b2d) —  ____________________________
                                   < The lobster in your shell. >
                                    ----------------------------
                                           \   ^__^
                                            \  (oo)\_______
```

### Actual

Same as expected.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — `src/cli/banner.test.ts` adds a test for the script-mode config path; previously `loadConfig` was not imported so the path always returned `undefined`.
- [x] Trace/log snippets — manual run output verified above.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: script returning a string via `execFileSync(cowsay)`, `resolveScriptTagline` called with the correct path, banner line rendered with multi-line tagline.
- Edge cases checked: script throws → empty tagline (no crash); `taglineScriptFile` not set when mode is `script` → empty tagline.
- What you did **not** verify: Windows paths with spaces; scripts that take >1 s (startup latency impact).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — new config keys; existing installs without them behave identically.
- Config/env changes? **Yes** — two new optional keys under `cli.banner`.
- Migration needed? **No**

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: set `cli.banner.taglineMode` back to `random` or `default`.
- Files/config to restore: `~/.openclaw/config.yaml` (remove `taglineMode`/`taglineScriptFile` keys).
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: banner hangs at startup (script with blocking I/O); unexpected output if script writes to stdout directly instead of returning a string.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: slow or hanging tagline script blocks CLI startup.
  - Mitigation: the feature is opt-in; users who experience this can unset `taglineScriptFile`. A timeout could be added in a follow-up.
- Risk: `loadConfig()` missing import (already present in old PR) silently suppresses the feature.
  - Mitigation: fixed in this PR; covered by new test in `banner.test.ts`.

target.title: fix(gateway): use default agent model for session defaults
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #50156.

`sessions.list.defaults` was resolving the fallback model from the global hardcoded default path, which ignored the default agent's effective `model.primary`. In Control UI this surfaced as a misleading extra "Default (...)" choice when a single configured agent had its own primary model.

This changes `getSessionDefaults()` to resolve through `resolveDefaultModelForAgent()` using the repo's default agent id, so the gateway reports the same primary model the default agent will actually run.

## Changes

- `src/gateway/session-utils.ts`: resolve session defaults from the default agent's effective model instead of the hardcoded global fallback path.
- `src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`: add a regression test covering a default agent with `model.primary = minimax/MiniMax-M2.5` and no global `agents.defaults.model.primary`.

## Test plan

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- Attempted: `pnpm test -- src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts -t "getSessionDefaults prefers the default agent primary model over the hardcoded fallback"`
  - Blocked locally by an existing dependency skew in this checkout: `node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-ai` is still linked to `0.54.1`, so the suite fails during import on `@mariozechner/pi-ai/oauth` before the test runs.

## AI-assisted

This PR was authored with AI assistance. I reviewed the change and verified the root cause in code before submitting.

target.title: fix: handle Windows-style session paths when running on POSIX
target.body:
## Summary

- When `sessions.json` is written by a Windows host but read inside a Linux Docker container, paths like `C:\Users\...\abc.jsonl` are treated as relative by `path.isAbsolute` (which only recognises POSIX absolutes)
- This causes `resolvePathWithinSessionsDir` to concatenate the Windows path onto the container base, producing broken paths like `/home/node/.openclaw/.../C:\Users\...\abc.jsonl`
- Adds `isWindowsAbsoluteOnPosix()` guard that detects drive-letter paths on POSIX and extracts the leaf filename, resolving it within the sessions directory

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify `resolvePathWithinSessionsDir` correctly extracts basename from `C:\Users\foo\sessions\abc.jsonl` on Linux
- [ ] Verify `C:/forward/slash/abc.jsonl` variant also handled
- [ ] Verify no behaviour change on native Windows (guard returns false when `path.sep === "\\"`)
- [ ] Verify normal POSIX relative paths still resolve correctly

target.title: feat: add skill usage telemetry — real-time exec-to-skill tracking
target.body:
## Problem

There is currently no way to distinguish between skills that are **actually used** (CLI executed) vs merely discovered (SKILL.md scanned at startup). Users with 30+ installed skills have no visibility into which ones bring real value.

Fixes #85263

## Solution

Instrument the exec-tool execution path to track skill invocations in real time.

### Changes

| File | Description |
|---|---|
| `src/agents/skills/skill-bins.ts` (new) | Builds skill→CLI binary mapping from loaded skills |
| `src/agents/skills/usage-tracker.ts` (new) | Telemetry module — atomic writes, in-memory cache, 1000-invocation cap per skill |
| `src/agents/skills/workspace.ts` (modified) | Exports `getSkillBinsMap()`, populated during skill loading |
| `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts` (modified) | Tracks skill invocations on exec success/failure |
| `src/cli/skills-cli.ts` (modified) | Adds `openclaw skills usage` subcommand with `--since`, `--agent`, `--json` options |

### How it works

1. Skills load → build bin→skill map from `requires.bins` / `requires.anyBins`
2. Each exec → extract first word of command, match against map
3. Match found → record to `~/.openclaw/agents/{agentId}/skill-usage.json`
4. View → `openclaw skills usage [--since 7d] [--agent xxx] [--json]`

### Known limitations

1. Only covers skills that declare `requires.bins` (CLI-based skills)
2. First-match wins when multiple skills share a binary
3. Backgrounded/yielded exec is not tracked
4. Does not correlate prior `read SKILL.md` with subsequent `exec` calls

### Verification

- `npx tsc --noEmit` passes with zero errors

target.title: fix(cli-runner): preserve claude-cli conversation history across session reseed
target.body:
## Summary
- **Problem:** When a `claude-cli` session is invalidated (auth-profile / auth-epoch rotation, system-prompt or MCP change), the fresh CLI run was reseeded with empty or one-sided history — the agent lost the entire prior conversation and started amnesiac.
- **Solution:** Let auth-boundary invalidations carry the raw transcript, force a reseed whenever a CLI session is invalidated, and fall back to the Claude CLI's own on-disk transcript when the OpenClaw-side transcript is missing or one-sided.
- **What changed:** `RAW_TRANSCRIPT_RESEED_ALLOWED_REASONS` (+auth-profile/auth-epoch); reseed-by-default on invalidation; tail-biased truncation; `MAX_CLI_SESSION_RESEED_HISTORY_CHARS` 12KB→64KB; new `loadClaudeCliReseedMessages` + `cliReseedMessagesLookThin`; fallback wiring in `prepare.ts`.
- **What did NOT change:** the compaction-summary reseed path; non-claude-cli CLI backends; the total agent timeout.

## Motivation
A routine auth-profile/auth-epoch rotation silently erased the user's entire conversation: the fresh CLI session started with zero context, re-asked questions and re-ran work. The full history still existed on disk (Claude CLI JSONL) but OpenClaw never read it back.

## Change Type
- [x] Bug fix

## Scope
- [x] Integrations (cli-runner / claude-cli)
- [x] Auth (auth-profile / auth-epoch invalidation path)

## Linked Issue/PR
Related: documents the `claude-cli` reseed path losing history. Checked: fixes a bug/regression.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior addressed:** claude-cli session loses all prior context after auth-profile/auth-epoch invalidation.
- **Real environment:** OpenClaw on WSL2 Ubuntu, claude-cli backend, Anthropic models.
- **Evidence:** the bound Claude CLI transcript for an affected session held only 2 user / 4 assistant turns while ~63 orphaned transcripts on disk held the real history (hundreds of turns); the OpenClaw session `.jsonl` held 57 assistant messages and 0 real user turns.
- **What was NOT tested:** a live patched-build end-to-end run (build is green, see below) — after-fix capture pending gateway restart onto the patched build.

## Root Cause
`loadCliSessionReseedMessages` only carried the raw transcript for reasons in `RAW_TRANSCRIPT_RESEED_ALLOWED_REASONS`, which excluded `auth-profile`/`auth-epoch`; raw reseed was also opt-in (`reseedFromRawTranscriptWhenUncompacted`, off by default). Separately, the reseed read the OpenClaw-side `.jsonl`, which for a claude-cli run holds only assistant outputs + runtime-event prompts — never the real user turns. No fallback reconstructed history from the authoritative Claude CLI JSONL.

## Regression Test Plan
`src/agents/cli-runner/session-history.test.ts`: covers auth-boundary raw reseed, tail-biased truncation, and `cliReseedMessagesLookThin` (empty / one-sided / two-sided / compaction-summary). 203 tests pass across the touched files.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
Continuity is preserved across CLI session invalidation. No UI/API surface change.

## Diagram
```
Before: invalidate(auth-profile) -> reseed = []           -> fresh session amnesiac
After:  invalidate(auth-profile) -> reseed from raw tail  -> if thin -> Claude CLI JSONL -> continuity
```

## Security Impact
- New permissions: No
- New secrets handling: No
- New network calls: No
- New exec surface: No
- New data access: reads `~/.claude/projects/<dir>/<cliSessionId>.jsonl` via the existing `readClaudeCliFallbackSeed` reader (already path-guarded; rejects path-like ids, enforces containment).

## Repro + Verification
- **Environment:** claude-cli backend, multi-turn session, no recent compaction.
- **Steps:** trigger an auth-profile/auth-epoch change, send the next message.
- **Expected:** fresh session keeps prior context.
- **Actual (before):** fresh session started with no history.

## Evidence
- [x] Failing-then-passing regression tests

## Human Verification
Verified: the new tests pass; full `pnpm build` is green; the diagnostic trace (orphaned-transcript counts, one-sided OpenClaw transcript) confirms the failure mode. NOT verified: a live patched-build end-to-end run (pending gateway restart).

## Review Conversations
- [x] Will resolve bot review threads before merge

## Compatibility / Migration
Backward compatible. No config or migration. The `reseedFromRawTranscriptWhenUncompacted` opt-in still works; it is now also implied on invalidation.

## Risks and Mitigations
- Larger reseed prompt (64KB cap, tail-biased) — bounded; mitigated by truncation keeping the freshest turns.
- Claude CLI JSONL fallback only triggers for `isClaudeCliProvider` + a resolved `claudeCliSessionId`, and only when the OpenClaw reseed is empty/one-sided — narrow blast radius.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(auth-profiles): break self-reinforcing session auth override loop (#85126)
target.body:
## Description

Fixes a self-reinforcing loop in `resolveSessionAuthProfileOverride` that permanently pins a session to the wrong auth profile after a fallback occurs.

### Root Cause

When a session falls back from its configured primary provider to a fallback (e.g., MiniMax → DeepSeek), the session entry stores the fallback provider's auth profile override (e.g., `deepseek:default`) with `source: "auto"`. On every subsequent turn, `resolveSessionAuthProfileOverride` builds the auth profile order exclusively from the **current run's provider** (the fallback, e.g., `deepseek`), never from the agent's configured **default model provider** (e.g., `minimax`). Since the fallback's auth profile is valid for the fallback provider's order, the clearing logic never fires, and the session is permanently stuck.

### Fix

When `source === "auto"` (the override was set automatically via fallback), also include the agent's configured default model provider's auth profiles in the order list by prepending them. This allows the `pickFirstAvailable` / `pickNextAvailable` logic to find and prefer the default model's auth profile when it's available, breaking the self-reinforcing loop.

### Testing

- For an agent configured with `minimax/MiniMax-M2.7` as primary and `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` as fallback:
  - Before: After the first fallback to DeepSeek, the session permanently pins to `deepseek:default`. Auth order is `["deepseek:default"]` → no alternative to pick.
  - After: Auth order becomes `["minimax:global", "deepseek:default"]`. On re-resolution, `pickFirstAvailable` finds `minimax:global` and switches back.

### Related

Fixes #85126 (supersedes the narrower issue description)
Related: #62412, #62710 (unmerged)

target.title: fix(gateway): deliver internal source replies to TUI
target.body: ## Summary - Fix message-tool source replies from internal OpenClaw chat surfaces so TUI/WebChat receive the visible reply in the active run instead of inheriting a stale external delivery route. - Keep the legacy tool-result/action channel as `webchat` for compatibility, and use `sourceReplySink: "internal-ui"` as the internal sink marker. - Keep external channels such as Telegram, Slack, and Discord as separate deliverable channels; this does not make their conversations visible in WebChat/TUI. - Split the source-reply display/mirror path into focused gateway helpers, add coverage for current-run routing, and remove PR-introduced dead exports (`isTuiClient`, `GatewayCurrentSessionClientId`, `GatewayInternalChatSurfaceClientId`). ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #38957 ## Design Boundary This PR intentionally does not create a new public channel, config key, CLI flag, provider policy, or external delivery path. `internal-ui` is an internal sink marker for message-tool source replies on OpenClaw-owned chat surfaces such as TUI, WebChat, Control UI, and native UI mode. The compatibility boundary is that `MessageActionRunResult.channel` and the model-facing result payload `channel` stay `webchat`. Existing agents, workflows, downstream parsers, and source copies that key on the historical message-tool `channel` value continue to see the old route label. New code should read `sourceReplySink: "internal-ui"` when it needs to know that the visible source reply was delivered into the OpenClaw-owned internal UI sink. ## Real behavior proof Behavior addressed: TUI-originated `message_tool_only` source replies render in the active TUI/current-run chat even when the session has a stale external delivery route, and the tool result keeps `channel: "webchat"` while identifying the visible sink with `sourceReplySink: "internal-ui"`. Real environment tested: Live local dev host on exact PR head `3027646d312f15eda341105b180ce206bbf32e68` (`OpenClaw 2026.5.19 (3027646)`), isolated staged OpenClaw home at `/tmp/openclaw-test-home-crK8kR`, foreground Gateway on `ws://127.0.0.1:18789`, throwaway loopback token auth, `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, `messages.visibleReplies: "message_tool"`, `gateway.controlUi.enabled: false`, bundled plugins from this worktree, live `openai/gpt-5.5` / `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, seeded session route `lastChannel: "telegram"` and `lastTo: "6101296751"` before the TUI-mode run. A full terminal TUI PTY proof was also run on the previous behavior head `088b030cf073f8731b74354b202faf0c17792f57`; the latest head only removes unused adapter exports and renames the internal source-reply facade to `Projector`. Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash PROOF_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-test-home-crK8kR SESSION=agent:main:tui-live-proof-terminal-202605201207 HOME="$PROOF_HOME" \ USERPROFILE="$PROOF_HOME" \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 \ OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST_QUIET=1 \ OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 \ OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=18789 \ node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run \ --port 18789 \ --bind loopback \ --auth token \ --token '' \ --allow-unconfigured \ --ws-log compact HOME="$PROOF_HOME" \ USERPROFILE="$PROOF_HOME" \ node scripts/run-node.mjs tui \ --url ws://127.0.0.1:18789 \ --token '' \ --session "$SESSION" \ --timeout-ms 180000 ``` In the real terminal TUI PTY, I typed and submitted: ```text Live routing proof from the real terminal TUI. You must use the message tool, not ordinary final text. Send exactly this text to the current TUI chat run: TUI TERMINAL LIVE PROOF TUI-TERM-1779278877-26056. No extra words, no markdown, no quotes. ``` On exact PR head `3027646d312f15eda341105b180ce206bbf32e68`, I ran a TUI-mode WebSocket proof client (`client.id: "openclaw-tui"`, `mode: "ui"`) against a separate seeded stale-route session, sent the same message-tool-only prompt through `chat.send`, waited for the live `chat` final event, and then queried `chat.history` over the Gateway WebSocket. Evidence after fix: The actual terminal TUI rendered this standalone assistant reply after the typed prompt: ```text TUI TERMINAL LIVE PROOF TUI-TERM-1779278877-26056 ``` The `chat.history` verifier for `agent:main:tui-live-proof-terminal-202605201207` returned: ```json { "ok": true, "sessionKey": "agent:main:tui-live-proof-terminal-202605201207", "historyContainsExpected": true, "messageCount": 4, "hasSourceReplySink": true, "hasCurrentRunTarget": true, "expected": "TUI TERMINAL LIVE PROOF TUI-TERM-1779278877-26056" } ``` The separate TUI-mode WebSocket proof used stale route `{ "lastChannel": "telegram", "lastTo": "6101296751" }`, skipped channels, and received a live chat final with run id `idem-tui-live-1779278321-8006:internal-source-reply:0`; its `chat.history` check returned `historyContainsExpected: true`. The exact-head TUI-mode WebSocket proof after the agent-requested design cleanup returned: ```json { "ok": true, "head": "3027646d312f15eda341105b180ce206bbf32e68", "client": { "id": "openclaw-tui", "mode": "ui" }, "sessionKey": "agent:main:tui-agent-design-proof-1779281522934", "runId": "idem-tui-agent-design-1779281522934", "staleRouteBefore": { "lastChannel": "telegram", "lastTo": "6101296751", "deliveryContext": { "channel": "telegram", "to": "6101296751" } }, "liveFinalRunId": "idem-tui-agent-design-1779281522934:internal-source-reply:0", "liveFinalText": "TUI AGENT DESIGN EXACT HEAD PROOF idem-tui-agent-design-1779281522934", "historyContainsExpected": true } ``` Observed result after fix: The message-tool source reply was visible in the active terminal TUI on the full PTY run, visible as a live `chat` final to the TUI-mode Gateway client on exact head `3027646d312f15eda341105b180ce206bbf32e68`, persisted into `chat.history`, and resolved to the current internal UI run despite the stale Telegram route and skipped external channels. The proof path would have had no working Telegram delivery fallback because channels were intentionally skipped. What was not tested: Live Telegram/Slack/Discord delivery was not rerun because this PR does not change their external delivery paths. The full terminal PTY proof was not repeated after the adapter-only constant extraction; exact-head proof used the same Gateway path with a TUI-mode client identity and verified the live `chat` final plus `chat.history`. Control UI static assets were disabled in the proof config to avoid an unrelated local `ui:build` startup delay. The throwaway proof token and temp proof home were not committed. Before evidence: Earlier base negative control on exact base commit `5e0850fc548ceab82af7244938b833024e485834` reproduced the failure shape: the model used the `message` tool and the source reply was persisted with `sourceReplySink: "internal-ui"`, but the active TUI returned to idle without rendering the standalone reply. ## Verification - `git diff --check` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/utils/message-channel.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.send-validation.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts` (6 files, 175 tests passed) - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.send-validation.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts` after the adapter cleanup (5 files, 175 tests passed) - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.send-validation.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts` after the agent-requested design cleanup (8 files, 384 tests passed) - `codex review --base origin/main` (clean; also ran `pnpm tsgo:prod` successfully during review) - `codex review --base origin/main` after the adapter cleanup (clean; no actionable findings) - `codex review --base origin/main` after the agent-requested design cleanup (clean; no actionable findings) - Dead-code audit: `git grep -n "\bisTuiClient\b\|\bGatewayCurrentSessionClientId\b\|\bGatewayInternalChatSurfaceClientId\b" -- . ':!node_modules' ':!dist' ':!coverage' || true` returned no matches - Exact-head live TUI-mode Gateway proof on `3027646d312f15eda341105b180ce206bbf32e68`, described above ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible: yes - Config/env changes: no - Public SDK/API changes: no - External channel behavior changes: no - Migration needed: no

target.title: feat(matrix): opt-in mention bypass for in-thread replies (threadBindings.bypassMentionInBoundThreads)
target.body:
Fixes #85111.

## Summary

Adds an opt-in `channels.matrix.threadBindings.bypassMentionInBoundThreads` (boolean, default false). When true, the matrix inbound handler skips the room-level `requireMention` gate for events that are replying inside a thread the bot already has prior activity in.

This enables the common bot UX of "mention to open a thread, free-form conversation continues inside it" on `requireMention` rooms — currently impossible because every in-thread reply that omits the mention is dropped as `skipping room message: no-mention`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: The matrix plugin's inbound handler drops in-thread replies in `requireMention: true` rooms even when the bot has been actively conversing in that thread. Users have to `@bot` on every line inside threads the bot opened, breaking the natural "mention to start, free-form continuation" UX. With this PR's opt-in flag, in-thread replies bypass the mention gate when the bot has a recorded session for the thread (via `recordInboundSession`'s thread-suffixed key) OR an explicit runtime binding (via `/focus`, `/acp bind`, etc.). Top-level messages keep `requireMention` enforcement; rooms without the flag set behave byte-identically to current `main`.

- **Real environment tested**: Unraid 7.2.5 host, `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest` (2026.5.19), Synapse via `ghcr.io/junkerderprovinz/matrix:latest` (AIO image), both containers on the same Docker bridge, Synapse Tailscale-served as `matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net`. Test room `parents` (`!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net`) configured under `channels.matrix.groups[<roomId>] = { requireMention: true, skills: [actual-budget, diagram-maker, news-summary] }`, three members (`@paulmeier`, `@openclaw`, `@admin`), E2EE on, `threadReplies: \"always\"`, `threadBindings: { enabled: true, idleHours: 24, spawnSessions: true, defaultSpawnContext: \"fork\" }`. The patched matrix monitor bundle (`/root/.openclaw/extensions/matrix/dist/monitor-4YYdKe_C.js`) was overlaid onto the running container via `docker cp`, then OpenClaw restarted. The unpatched bundle was preserved at `monitor-4YYdKe_C.js.bak` for revert.

- **Exact steps or command run after the patch**: Live-environment verification — overlay the patched matrix monitor bundle onto the running container, restart, then exercise the in-thread reply path in `#parents`:

  ```
  $ ssh root@<unraid> 'docker cp OpenClaw:/root/.openclaw/extensions/matrix/dist/monitor-4YYdKe_C.js /tmp/monitor-orig.js'
  $ scp root@<unraid>:/tmp/monitor-orig.js /tmp/monitor-orig.js
  $ python3 -c \"src=open('/tmp/monitor-orig.js').read();
    old='const shouldBypassMention = allowTextCommands && isRoom && shouldRequireMention && !wasMentioned && !hasExplicitMention && commandAuthorized && hasControlCommandInMessage;';
    new='const inThreadReply = threadRootId !== undefined && threadRootId !== messageId; let threadSessionExists = false; if (inThreadReply) { try { const _storePath = core.channel.session.resolveStorePath(cfg.session?.store, { agentId: _route.agentId }); const _ts = core.channel.session.readSessionUpdatedAt({ storePath: _storePath, sessionKey: _route.sessionKey }); threadSessionExists = _ts !== undefined && _ts !== null; } catch (_e) { threadSessionExists = false; } } const inExistingBoundThread = inThreadReply && (threadSessionExists || _runtimeBindingId !== null); const shouldBypassMention = isRoom && shouldRequireMention && !wasMentioned && !hasExplicitMention && ((allowTextCommands && commandAuthorized && hasControlCommandInMessage) || inExistingBoundThread);';
    open('/tmp/monitor-patched.js','w').write(src.replace(old, new, 1))\"
  $ scp /tmp/monitor-patched.js root@<unraid>:/tmp/
  $ ssh root@<unraid> 'docker cp /tmp/monitor-patched.js OpenClaw:/root/.openclaw/extensions/matrix/dist/monitor-4YYdKe_C.js && docker restart OpenClaw'
  ```

  In `#parents`, posted `@openclaw ping` (top-level mention; opens a thread), then in that thread posted `hello there` (no mention).

  Runtime acceptance-criteria suite — runtime executed locally against the patched branch (`308da8c5`):

  ```
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/direct-room.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/direct-management.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/direct.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test.ts \\
      extensions/matrix/src/matrix/send/targets.test.ts
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**: Live patched-runtime gateway log on the deployment, redacted — bot processing the in-thread reply that was previously dropped. After the user's mention of \`@openclaw\` opens the thread, the in-thread \`hello there\` follow-up triggers an outbound encrypted-message burst (Olm session prepares for the typing indicator + final reply) in the parents room. No \`skipping room message\` entries fire for the in-thread events:

  ```
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:43:08.376-05:00\" \"matrix: logged in as @openclaw:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.376-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648376.1: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.464-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648464.2: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.537-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648537.3: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
  \"time\":\"2026-05-22T05:44:08.614-05:00\" \"[MatrixClient.[!pgXEKemZcQTfeiaNvM:matrix.<tailnet>.ts.net encryption]] m1779446648614.4: Checking for missing Olm sessions\"
   (+ 7 more bursts of streaming-quiet typing updates ending at m1779446649228.11)
  ```

  11 outbound encrypted-message preparations in the same thread within 853 ms — the streaming-quiet typing-indicator pattern fires throughout the agent turn, then a final reply. Compare to the pre-patch deployment captured earlier in #85111's reproduction, where the same in-thread message produced a `\"message\":\"skipping room message\"` entry at `handler.ts:1032` with no outbound activity.

  Runtime acceptance suite (verbose) against `308da8c5` — the three behaviors this PR is responsible for:

  ```
   ✓ monitor/handler > createMatrixRoomMessageHandler > bypasses requireMention for in-thread replies when a session record already exists for the thread
   ✓ monitor/handler > createMatrixRoomMessageHandler > bypasses requireMention for in-thread replies when an explicit runtime binding exists (e.g. /focus)
   ✓ monitor/handler > createMatrixRoomMessageHandler > still drops in-thread replies without mention when bypassMentionInBoundThreads is off (default)
     (+ 107 other existing handler behaviors, all green)

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  110 passed (110)
     Duration  2.92s
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: On the live patched deployment, an in-thread `hello there` (no `@openclaw` mention) inside the thread the bot just opened triggers the agent — Element shows `OpenClaw is typing…`, the user's message gets the `👀` ack reaction, the bot replies inside the same thread. Pre-patch, the identical message was logged as `skipping room message` and never reached the agent. Top-level non-mention messages in the same room continue to be dropped (legacy `requireMention` behavior preserved). With the new flag off (the default for everyone who doesn't opt in), behavior is byte-identical to current `main`, and the existing regression at `handler.test.ts:1617` (`does not refresh bound Matrix thread bindings for room messages dropped before routing`) continues to pass without modification.

- **What was not tested**: The unmodified-image runtime (i.e. with `bypassMentionInBoundThreads: true` set in `openclaw.json` and a CI-rebuilt `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest` carrying the patch through the official build pipeline). The live proof above uses an in-place bundle overlay on a running container, which exercises the patched mention-gate code path correctly but uses the always-on form of the discriminator (the running runtime has no way to read the new opt-in config field). The flag-gated form is what the source PR ships; the gate evaluation is covered by the three handler tests above, two of which exercise the bypass path and one of which exercises the legacy-default-off path. Maintainer-side CI rebuild will produce the final runtime artifact that exercises the flag-gated form end-to-end.

## Discriminator update vs the originally-pushed version

The first commit on this branch (`6f2a5b6e`) gated the bypass on `_runtimeBindingId !== null` only. Live testing surfaced that natural per-thread continuation does NOT produce a runtime binding — `sessionBindingService.resolveByConversation` returns `null` for threads that were opened by the bot replying, only for threads that have an explicit ACP/user-managed binding (via `/focus`, `/acp bind`, etc.). The handler tests in `6f2a5b6e` mocked `resolveByConversation` to return a binding, which masked the wrong discriminator: tests passed because the mock matched the broken code path, not because the real-world code path worked.

The current commit (`308da8c5`) replaces the single-signal check with an OR over two signals:

1. **Session-store record exists for the thread** — via `core.channel.session.readSessionUpdatedAt({ storePath, sessionKey: _route.sessionKey })`. The thread session key is set by `resolveMatrixInboundRoute` whenever `thread.threadId` is populated (always under `threadReplies: \"inbound\"`/`\"always\"`), and `recordInboundSession` persists it on the first message the bot processes in the thread. So this is the natural-continuation path.
2. **Explicit runtime binding exists** — the original `_runtimeBindingId !== null` check, kept as a parallel signal for the `/focus`-bound case.

The session-store path is the dominant case in real deployments; the runtime-binding path covers explicit-binding flows for parity. Either signal is sufficient.

## Files changed

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `extensions/matrix/src/config-schema.ts` | New `bypassMentionInBoundThreads` field in `matrixThreadBindingsSchema` |
| `extensions/matrix/src/types.ts` | Same field added to `MatrixThreadBindingsConfig` TS type |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/index.ts` | Read `accountConfig.threadBindings?.bypassMentionInBoundThreads === true` and pass into handler params |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.ts` | Handler params accept the flag; in-thread-reply discriminator computes session-store existence via `readSessionUpdatedAt`; OR with `_runtimeBindingId` and feeds `shouldBypassMention` |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test.ts` | Three tests: session-record bypass, runtime-binding bypass, legacy default-off drop |
| `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test-helpers.ts` | Test harness option `bypassMentionInBoundThreads` |
| `docs/channels/matrix.md` | Documents the new field plus discriminators and the preserved legacy contract |

## Related

- #85017 / #85018 — DM classification veto on `is_direct: false` (companion PR; same `#parents`/`#adults` deployment thread).
- #85111 — the filed issue with full reproduction.

target.title: feat: add auto fast mode cutoff
target.body:
Fixes #85087

## Summary

- adds `fast: auto` alongside `fast: on/off`, with the default 60s cutoff and configurable `fast_seconds`
- resolves effective fast mode dynamically so follow-up model calls after the cutoff can run without priority/fast tier where the backend supports retiering
- emits channel-visible fast progress notices in the tool/progress stream: `💨Fast: auto-off(...)` at the next tool-execution boundary after the cutoff, then `💨Fast: auto-on` at turn reset
- shows the current effective fast mode directly in Telegram `/fast`, including the configured auto threshold in status text and the native menu button
- keeps Telegram progress logs visible when `streaming.progress.keepLog` is enabled
- updates TUI/UI/ACP/protocol/docs/tests for the new auto mode

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/config-schema.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts src/agents/fast-mode.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.fast-mode-auto.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-usage.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/slash-commands.browser-import.test.ts`
- `pnpm --config.verify-deps-before-run=false test:bundled`
- `pnpm --config.verify-deps-before-run=false protocol:check`
- `pnpm --config.verify-deps-before-run=false ui:i18n:check`
- `pnpm --config.verify-deps-before-run=false check:architecture`
- `pnpm --config.verify-deps-before-run=false build`
- `pnpm --config.verify-deps-before-run=false openclaw gateway restart`
- `pnpm --config.verify-deps-before-run=false openclaw gateway status`
- Live Telegram Desktop proof via Computer Use against the managed LaunchAgent gateway from this checkout
- Earlier broad local `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-swiftlint:$PATH" pnpm --config.verify-deps-before-run=false check:changed` passed through conflict markers, changelog attributions, dependency/package guards, core/core-test/extension/extension-test typecheck, core/extensions/scripts lint; local `lint apps` failed because portable SwiftLint cannot load SourceKit without full Xcode on this host

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `fast: auto` starts with effective fast mode enabled, disables fast mode for later model calls after the configured threshold, reports the cutoff in channel progress, resets with `💨Fast: auto-on`, and shows current fast status in Telegram `/fast` without requiring `/fast status`.

Real environment tested: local managed Gateway LaunchAgent, CLI/Gateway `2026.5.21`, Telegram plugin in a real Telegram Desktop DM, config `fastMode: auto` with `fast_seconds: 15`, real provider `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, and shell tool calls around an ~18s sleep.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm --config.verify-deps-before-run=false build
pnpm --config.verify-deps-before-run=false openclaw gateway restart
pnpm --config.verify-deps-before-run=false openclaw gateway status
```

Telegram mixed-order prompt used after restart:

```text
Run exactly three shell tool calls, one at a time, wait for each output before next: first command `python3 -c 'print("tool_a_before")'`; second command `python3 -c 'import time; s=int(time.time()*1000); time.sleep(18); e=int(time.time()*1000); print(f"sleep_start_ms={s}"); print(f"sleep_end_ms={e}"); print(f"sleep_duration_ms={e-s}")'`; third command `python3 -c 'print("tool_b_after")'`. Do not combine commands. Do not use loops. Final reply exactly: tool_a_before, observed sleep_duration_ms, tool_b_after.
```

Evidence after fix: Telegram Desktop showed one progress draft containing normal shell tool lines before `💨Fast: auto-off(32s>=15s)`, then more normal shell tool lines before `💨Fast: auto-on`. The final Telegram reply reported `tool_a_before`, `sleep_duration_ms=18003`, and `tool_b_after`. Screenshots are intentionally not attached here because the live Telegram window contains PII; reviewer/maintainer can attach a redacted screenshot separately.

Observed result after fix: gateway status probe was ok on port `18789`, the managed LaunchAgent command pointed at this checkout's `dist/index.js`, the progress draft kept the fast events in the tool list, and the final reply arrived after all tool results.

What was not tested: local SwiftLint app lint, because portable SwiftLint on this host fails to load `sourcekitdInProc.framework` without full Xcode. CI macOS should cover that lane. Config hot reload for normal Telegram reply dispatch remains future work; per reviewer/user direction, this PR does not add per-message config rereads. In a real provider turn, `💨Fast: auto-on` is the end-of-turn reset event, so it is not expected to have a later tool line in the same turn; the Telegram renderer unit test covers the synthetic mixed-draft order where both fast callbacks are surrounded by ordinary tool lines.

target.title: chore: harden npm shrinkwrap release path
target.body:
"## Summary
- Ship the root `npm-shrinkwrap.json` plus regenerated plugin shrinkwrap/package-lock metadata so dependency pins and integrity hashes are reviewed in git.
- Add plugin npm package hardening so suitable OpenClaw-owned plugin packages can publish explicit `bundledDependencies`.
- Add a package-level opt-out for native-heavy plugins; Codex and ACP opt out because universal bundling would ship every platform binary to every user.
- Document shrinkwrap, plugin dependency resolution, bundled dependency tradeoffs, and native-heavy opt-outs in the gateway security and plugin dependency docs.

## Explainer
This keeps two separate hardening layers:

- `npm-shrinkwrap.json`: npm's publishable lockfile. It pins resolved versions and integrity hashes for npm consumers, unlike `package-lock.json`, which is normally ignored when the package is installed as a dependency.
- `bundledDependencies`: npm's packaging mechanism for including selected dependency package folders inside the tarball. This can make plugin installs more offline/portable, but it is not free.

For normal OpenClaw-owned npm plugins, the package manifest augmentation can publish runtime dependencies as bundled dependencies. That preserves the tested dependency tree inside the plugin tarball.

For Codex and ACP, that is the wrong default. Force-collecting skipped optional native packages made Codex about 561.7 MB packed and 1.45 GB unpacked because it carried every OpenAI native binary for every OS/arch. ACP would similarly force-collect all-platform Claude Agent SDK, Codex ACP, and esbuild optional packages. Both plugins now set `openclaw.release.bundleRuntimeDependencies: false`, so they still publish shrinkwrap metadata, but npm resolves the host-appropriate native dependency set at install time.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/plugin-npm-package-manifest.test.ts`: passed, 7 tests.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/package-manager-config.test.ts src/infra/update-global.test.ts`: passed, 34 tests.
- `pnpm deps:shrinkwrap:check`: passed after rebasing onto `origin/main` and refreshing shrinkwrap metadata for `2026.5.21`.
- `pnpm check:docs`: passed.
- `pnpm check:architecture`: passed after rebasing onto latest `origin/main` and extracting the plugin registry snapshot source type into a leaf `.types` module.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/plugin-npm-package-manifest.test.ts`: passed after switching bundled plugin dependency staging to `npm ci` so package-local `npm-shrinkwrap.json` is honored.
- `git diff --check`: passed.
- `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`: clean; no accepted/actionable findings after ACP opt-out and after the architecture-cycle fix.
- Codex pack with `OPENCLAW_PLUGIN_NPM_BUNDLE_DEPENDENCIES=1`: `size=199709`, `unpacked=978628`, `bundled=0`, native bundled packages `(none)`.
- ACP pack with `OPENCLAW_PLUGIN_NPM_BUNDLE_DEPENDENCIES=1`: `size=50189`, `unpacked=202060`, `bundled=0`.
- Slack pack with `OPENCLAW_PLUGIN_NPM_BUNDLE_DEPENDENCIES=1`: `size=3981905`, `unpacked=19825530`, `bundled=114`, proving normal plugin bundling still applies.
- Local Codex install from the opt-out tarball: `Installed plugin: codex`; plugin list showed `status=loaded`, dependency status installed/requiredInstalled/optionalInstalled all true, and npm installed `@openai/codex` plus host-native `@openai/codex-darwin-arm64`.
- GitHub Actions on previous head `6498ca35637692d3e8e02335eb2f0ee6a96c1425`: CI `26279288545`, Blacksmith Testbox `26279288602`, Blacksmith Build Artifacts Testbox `26279288543`, CodeQL `26279288601`, CodeQL Critical Quality `26279288557`, Workflow Sanity `26279288494`, OpenGrep PR Diff `26279288552`, Real behavior proof `26279287882`, and Dependency Change Awareness `26279287884` all passed. Current rebased head `c41e96df11` is queued after the shrinkwrap-honoring bundled install fix.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: npm install hardening now supports reviewed shrinkwrap pins and optional bundled plugin runtime dependencies without forcing native-heavy plugins like Codex and ACP to ship every platform binary.

Real environment tested: local macOS source checkout, Node/pnpm repo runtime, OpenClaw plugin install flow against a packed Codex npm plugin tarball, ACP and Slack package measurements, latest-main architecture shard, plus current-head GitHub Actions CI/Testbox proof.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: packed Codex and ACP with `OPENCLAW_PLUGIN_NPM_BUNDLE_DEPENDENCIES=1`, installed the resulting `openclaw-codex-2026.5.20.tgz` via `pnpm openclaw plugins install npm-pack:<tarball> --force --dangerously-force-unsafe-install`, then ran `pnpm openclaw plugins list --json` against the isolated state directory. After rebasing onto `origin/main`, regenerated shrinkwrap metadata and reran the exact failing CI shards locally before pushing `aa8584bee8664572e8c14f63b4e64d079a523d41`; ACP opt-out plus latest-main architecture-cycle follow-ups are included in current head `c41e96df11`.

Evidence after fix: Codex pack output reported `bundled=0`, `size=199709`, `unpacked=978628`; ACP pack output reported `bundled=0`, `size=50189`, `unpacked=202060`; Codex install output reported `Installed plugin: codex`; plugin dependency status was fully installed; the install root contained `@openai/codex` and `@openai/codex-darwin-arm64`, not the full cross-platform binary set. `pnpm check:architecture` and `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/plugin-npm-package-manifest.test.ts` pass on latest `origin/main`; current-head CI/Testbox workflows are queued.

Observed result after fix: Codex no longer produces the 561.7 MB universal native-binary tarball, ACP stays a tiny host-resolved tarball, while non-opted-out plugins such as Slack still publish bundled runtime dependencies.

What was not tested: actual npm publish and current-head cross-OS Codex/ACP install after the opt-out changes.
"

target.title: Fix memory_search wiki fallback and plugin tool registry reuse
target.body:
## Summary
- add a direct memory-wiki fallback for `memory_search` and `memory_get` when wiki corpus supplements are missing at runtime
- avoid mutating global plugin memory state during plugin tool resolution
- add regression coverage for wiki fallback and gateway-bindable registry reuse

## Problem
`memory_search(corpus="wiki")` could return no results even while `wiki_search` worked, because scoped activating plugin loads could wipe process-global memory wiki supplement registrations. Tool resolution could also over-reuse a gateway-bindable active registry that was not actually compatible with the requested plugin set.

## Fix
- add a direct `memory-wiki` fallback inside `memory-core`
- change plugin tool resolution to reuse only compatible active registries, otherwise perform a non-activating snapshot load

## Validation
- targeted Vitest coverage for memory-core wiki fallback
- targeted Vitest coverage for plugin tool registry reuse

target.title: Memory: validate dreaming cron frequency
target.body:
## Summary
- Validate `dreaming.frequency` up front so an invalid cron expression no longer fails silently later in the managed cron system.
- Adds `validateMemoryDreamingFrequency()` to the memory host SDK; returns a discriminated `{ valid }` result with a `reason: "inline-timezone" | "parse" | "timezone"` so callers can tailor guidance.
- Validator explicitly checks the IANA timezone via `Intl.DateTimeFormat` and forces `cron.nextRun(new Date())` after construction to defeat Croner's lazy-construction short-circuit.
- `extensions/memory-core` calls the validator before creating/updating the managed dreaming cron job. On invalid input it logs an error and returns `{ status: "invalid", removed: 0 }`, intentionally leaving the last-known-good managed job untouched so dreaming keeps running until the config is fixed.
- Inline `TZ=`/`CRON_TZ=` expressions get a targeted hint: `Timezone must be set via dreaming.timezone, not inline in the expression.` Plain parse errors do not.

## Test plan
- [x] `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/memory-host-sdk/dreaming.ts src/memory-host-sdk/dreaming.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts`
- [x] `corepack pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- [x] `corepack pnpm test src/memory-host-sdk/dreaming.test.ts`
- [x] `corepack pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts`
- New tests cover: valid/invalid frequency validation, inline-timezone reason, invalid IANA timezone, plain-parse error does not surface timezone guidance, pre-existing managed cron job is preserved when the new config is invalid.

target.title: feat(memory-core): Memory v2 foundation — sidecar, ingest, rerank (all default-off)
target.body:
Additive foundation for a SQLite-backed Memory v2 sidecar and two pipelines
(ingest, rerank). Every surface is wired behind a plugin-config flag and
defaults to **off**, so this PR is behaviorally a no-op until a workspace
opts in via `memoryV2.ingest.enabled` / `memoryV2.rerank.enabled`.

## Why

Memory v2 aims to let `memory-core` track per-location signals (salience,
pinned/superseded status, last-access recency) alongside the existing
markdown files, and rerank `memory_search` results with those signals. This
PR lands the foundation without changing existing behavior.

## Slices (6 commits, intentionally atomic)

1. **Slice 0 — Sidecar foundation** (`2523e5a7`)
   `memory/v2-sidecar.db` schema + repo + `openSidecarDatabase`. No
   consumers yet.

2. **Slice 1a — Ingest internals** (`d1e9f3b0`)
   Dedupe, extract, handler, normalize, secret-filter, synthetic-ref.
   Pure module; not registered.

3. **Slice 1b — Ingest wiring** (`eb3d250e`)
   `registerMemoryV2Ingest(api)` subscribes to `agent_end`. Reads
   `memoryV2.ingest.enabled` via a type-guarded config check; returns a
   noop subscription when the flag is off.

4. **Slice 1.5 — Location keys + shadow-touch helper** (`1d33d0ad`)
   Canonical `memoryLocationId` / path normalization, `recordTouchedLocations`
   helper. Prerequisite for both rerank lookup and future shadow-row writes.

5. **Slice 2a — Rerank module** (`a1319c00`)
   `buildRerankWrapper`, `applyRerank`, `loadSidecarSignalsByLocations`.
   Pure module; not wired.

6. **Slice 2b — Rerank wiring** (`1e4431b6`)
   `createMemorySearchTool` gains an optional `rerank?: RerankFn`. When the
   plugin config enables rerank, `buildMemoryV2Rerank(api)` returns a
   `RerankFn` that is threaded into the tool factory. When off, the rerank
   field isn't even spread into the options bag — the call path is
   byte-identical to before.

## Safety properties

- **Default off**, enforced by explicit `=== true` check in both wirings.
  Truthy coercion (`1`, `"true"`) does not enable.
- **Type-guarded config reads** — malformed `memoryV2.*` never throws;
  it silently degrades to disabled.
- **Identity fallback on any rerank error** — `buildRerankWrapper` wraps
  its sidecar db calls in `try/catch` and returns original results on
  failure. A broken sidecar cannot break `memory_search`.
- **Per-workspace db cache** inside the wrapper closure; one `DatabaseSync`
  per `workspaceDir` per plugin activation.
- **Additive manifest** — `memoryV2.rerank` is a new object under the
  sibling `memoryV2.ingest` block added in Slice 1b; no existing keys
  changed.

## Upstream / update-survival notes

The hunk most likely to cause future rebase friction is the 13-line
pre-sort block in `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts` (the `merged`/
`reranked` intermediate between result-merge and `.toSorted(...)`). That
area of the file has seen active upstream work (#67277, #65068). The hunk
is additive and flag-gated, so a conflict resolution is mechanical:
re-inject the `merged` intermediate and the conditional `reranked` call.

The rest of the surface is either:
- Low-churn (`openclaw.plugin.json` interior addition, `tools.test.ts`
  end-of-file append), or
- Contained in new files under `src/memory-v2/` that have no upstream
  counterpart.

## Known follow-ups (explicitly out of scope here)

- Seam hardening for `api.pluginConfig: unknown`, which is declared as a
  local type in both `ingest-wiring.ts` and `rerank-wiring.ts`. If the
  SDK renames the field, both wirings silently see `undefined` and stay
  disabled. A future small PR can replace the local type with the real
  SDK register-API type.
- "Shadow-only" mode: currently `shadowOnRecall` only runs when rerank
  itself is enabled. Collecting training signal before flipping rerank
  on would need a separate path.
- Cosmetic: `tools.ts:304` uses `merged as never` to bridge the union
  element type to `RerankableResult`. `as readonly RerankableResult[]`
  would be clearer. Not included to keep this PR scoped to feature work.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts` → 62 files / 643 passed / 3 skipped, 0 failed
- [x] `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/memory-core/tsconfig.json --noEmit` → clean
- [x] `pnpm exec oxlint` on all touched files → 0 warnings, 0 errors
- [x] `pnpm check` (full repo gate: no-conflict-markers, tool-display, host-env-policy:swift, import-cycles, madge-import-cycles, tsgo, extension-package-boundary, lint, webhook/auth lints) → all 11 gates clean

target.title: fix(mattermost): support filePath and path as mediaUrl fallbacks in handleAction
target.body:
## Summary

The Mattermost `handleAction` only checked `params.media` for media URL, ignoring `filePath` and `path` which are commonly used by agents. This is inconsistent with other channel handlers like Discord and Google Chat.

## Problem

When an agent uses the message tool with `filePath` or `path` instead of `media`:
- Discord handler: supports `media` → `path` → `filePath` fallback
- Google Chat handler: supports `media` → `filePath` → `path` fallback  
- Mattermost handler: only checks `media` → file upload fails silently

## Fix

Add fallback chain for media URL resolution:
1. `params.media`
2. `params.filePath`
3. `params.path`

## Related

This is a partial fix for #67784. The other parts (threadId and mediaLocalRoots) are already addressed by:
- PR #52120 (thread context inheritance)
- PR #58439 (mediaLocalRoots forwarding)

## Test Plan
- [x] Existing unit tests pass
- [x] Lint passes

Closes openclaw#67784 (filePath/path fallback only)

target.title: Update install.sh
target.body: Is there any reason to use the starting string _linux-gnu_ in the **_detect_os_or_die_** method? I'm using openSUSE, and OSTYPE variable returns the string _linux_ I can change the variable by running the `OSTYPE=linux-gnu` command, but maybe it could be fixed this way. ___ Before installing OpenClaw, you should install the required libraries: `sudo zypper install curl, nodejs, git` In this particular case, openSUSE comes with _curl_ preinstalled, but some Linux distros don't. So, the people using those distros will have to use their package managers anyway before starting the installation of OpenClaw. After that, if you try to run the next command with the current state of the _install.sh_ file `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash` you'll get the following error (which doesn't give any clue about how to solve it) But if you apply the change I'm proposing, and run the previous command, the installation will end successfully in a few minutes, and finally the setup process will start.

target.title: fix(feishu): target typing reaction on inbound
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: In Feishu groups with Topic mode (or `replyInThread`) enabled, the typing-indicator emoji reaction landed on the topic/thread root message instead of the user's inbound message. - Why it matters: User-visible confusion — the reaction did not correspond to the message being processed. - What changed: Threaded `inboundMessageId` (`ctx.messageId`) through `handleFeishuMessage` into `createFeishuReplyDispatcher`; `addTypingIndicator` now uses `inboundMessageId ?? replyToMessageId` so the reaction targets the user's actual message, with safe fallback. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Reply target, thread routing, outbound message metadata, non-group DM flows, webhook mode. Currently when user reply in a thread the typing reaction is applied on the main thread message Changed it to apply the typing react on the message that user sent int he thread ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** In a Feishu group with Topic mode enabled, the bot's Typing emoji reaction landed on the topic / thread root message instead of the message the user just sent inside the topic. - **Real environment tested:** Live Feishu (Lark) group with Topic mode enabled, running the patched OpenClaw CLI against a real Feishu app over the WebSocket transport. Host: macOS 15, Node 22, pnpm 10.32.1. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 1. Built the patched branch locally and started OpenClaw against the real Feishu app: `pnpm openclaw channels feishu run`. 2. Inside an existing topic in a Feishu group, @mentioned the bot with a new message. 3. Watched the bot's Typing emoji reaction land in the Lark UI on the message that had just been sent. - **Evidence after fix:** Lark UI screenshot taken on the same group after applying this patch — the Typing emoji reaction now attaches to the user's just-sent message inside the topic instead of the topic root. ![After fix — Typing reaction targets the user's inbound message inside the topic](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9ebce304-e8f0-4b09-a7cb-2e58ad3f1ac3) For comparison, before the fix the reaction landed on the thread root: ![Before fix — Typing reaction lands on the topic root](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/94dbdceb-475f-4d2a-a9c9-8148cc9783bb) - **Observed result after fix:** The Typing emoji reaction is rendered on the message the user just sent inside the topic. The topic / thread root no longer receives the keepalive reaction. Behavior in 1:1 DMs and non-topic groups is unchanged. - **What was not tested:** Webhook delivery mode (only the WebSocket transport was exercised live). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - N/A - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause - Root cause: In `createFeishuReplyDispatcher`, the typing reaction used `replyToMessageId` directly. target.title: fix: remove truncated preview from inbound system events
target.body:
## Summary

Removes the truncated 160-char message preview from system event headers for Slack, MS Teams, and Mattermost channels.

## Problem

System events were including a truncated preview of the message body:

```
System: [2026-04-15 21:56:40 MDT] Slack DM from Jared: What I'm saying is that you are can handle this task (you just showed me you can), but starting tomorrow (and including tomorrow), When we're planning the day o
```

This caused the model to sometimes reference the truncated header instead of the full message body, leading to responses like "your message got cut off at 'day o'" when the full message was delivered correctly.

## Solution

System events now only contain the notification label without message content:

```
System: [2026-04-15 21:56:40 MDT] Slack DM from Jared
```

The system event's purpose is notification ("a message arrived"), not content delivery. The full message body is delivered separately in the user turn.

## Changes

- `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts` — remove preview from system event (keep for debug logging/type compat)
- `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler/message-handler.ts` — remove preview from system event (keep for debug logging)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` — remove preview from system event

## Testing

- `pnpm test:extension slack` — ✅ 641 tests passed
- `pnpm test:extension msteams` — ✅ 862 tests passed
- `pnpm test:extension mattermost` — ✅ 316 passed (1 timeout failure unrelated to changes)
- `pnpm build` — ✅
- Pre-commit hooks — ✅

Fixes #67503

target.title: feat(messages): add botDebounceMs to inbound debounce config
target.body:
## Problem

When two AI agents share a channel, they can exchange messages faster than any human — burning tokens and generating noise. The existing `messages.inbound.debounceMs` config applies uniformly to all senders; there is no way to enforce a longer cooldown specifically for bot-originated messages.

Self-enforcement at the agent level is fragile. Platform-level control is the correct fix.

## Solution

Add a `botDebounceMs` field to the `messages.inbound` config schema. When set, this value is used instead of `debounceMs` whenever the inbound message originates from a bot or agent (`senderIsBot: true`).

Priority chain (unchanged for non-bot senders):

```
overrideMs → byChannel → botDebounceMs (if senderIsBot) → debounceMs → 0
```

## Changes

### `src/config/types.messages.ts`
Add `botDebounceMs?: number` to `InboundDebounceConfig` with JSDoc.

### `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts`
Add `botDebounceMs: z.number().int().nonnegative().optional()` to `InboundDebounceSchema`.

### `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts`
Extend `resolveInboundDebounceMs` with an optional `senderIsBot?: boolean` param. When true, `botDebounceMs` is slotted between `byChannel` and `base` in the priority chain. Fully backward-compatible — omitting `senderIsBot` preserves existing behavior.

### `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.test.ts` _(new)_
Unit tests covering all priority chain cases:
- Base debounceMs
- byChannel override
- botDebounceMs applies when senderIsBot is true
- botDebounceMs ignored when senderIsBot is false or omitted
- overrideMs always wins
- byChannel wins over botDebounceMs
- Graceful fallback when botDebounceMs is unset

## Call site scope

This PR wires the resolver logic and config schema. The `senderIsBot` param defaults to `undefined` (treated as `false`), so existing behavior is fully preserved. Channel adapter call-site updates (passing `senderIsBot` from provider-native bot flags: Slack `is_bot`, Discord `bot`, Telegram `is_bot`) are intentionally deferred to a follow-up PR to keep this diff reviewable. That follow-up will thread the flag through the relevant channel handlers.

## Config example

```json
{
  "messages": {
    "inbound": {
      "debounceMs": 500,
      "botDebounceMs": 10000
    }
  }
}
```

Human messages: 500ms debounce. Bot/agent messages: 10s minimum before triggering a reply.

## Notes

- `byChannel` overrides win over `botDebounceMs` even when `senderIsBot` is true (see priority chain). If you set a per-channel burst window, it takes precedence. This is documented in the `InboundDebounceConfig` type JSDoc.
- Scope is intentionally minimal: ~5 lines of logic change, backward-compatible, fully tested.

target.title: fix(agents): detect Cloudflare challenge pages and provide actionable error message
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Cloudflare returns 403 with JS Challenge page when `openai-codex` provider accessed via proxy from mainland China. Current error message says "Authentication failed" which is misleading — the issue is TLS fingerprint detection, not auth.
- **Why it matters:** Users waste time re-authenticating when the real fix is a reverse proxy with browser-like TLS fingerprint.
- **What changed:** Added `cloudflare_challenge` failure kind to detect Cloudflare challenge pages (403 with `cf-browser-verification`, `challenges.cloudflare.com`, etc.) and return actionable error message explaining TLS fingerprinting + reverse proxy workaround.
- **What did NOT change:** Generic 403 HTML errors still classified as `auth_html_403`. No changes to transport layer, provider config, or TLS implementation.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #67670
- Related #67642 (Cloudflare HTML error classification for 5xx)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** `classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind` classifies all 403 HTML responses as `auth_html_403` without checking for Cloudflare-specific challenge patterns. Cloudflare challenge pages contain distinctive markers (`cf-browser-verification`, `challenges.cloudflare.com`) that distinguish them from genuine auth failures.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No pattern matching for Cloudflare challenge page signatures before falling through to generic 403 HTML classification.
- **Contributing context:** Issue #67642 fixed Cloudflare 5xx HTML classification but didn't address 403-specific challenge pages.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- **Coverage level that should have caught this:**
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/provider-error-patterns.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** 403 HTML with Cloudflare challenge patterns (`cf-browser-verification`, `challenges.cloudflare.com`, `_cf_chl_`, `just a moment`, `ray id:`) returns `cloudflare_challenge` failure kind, not `auth_html_403`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** Unit test directly verifies classification logic with minimal HTML fixture containing only Cloudflare challenge markers.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** None (new functionality).
- **If no new test is added, why not:** N/A (3 new tests added).

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

**Before:** Users seeing Cloudflare 403 challenges get misleading error:
> "Authentication failed with an HTML 403 response from the provider. Re-authenticate and verify your provider account access."

**After:** Users get actionable error explaining root cause and workaround:
> "Cloudflare blocked the request with a browser challenge (HTTP 403). Node.js TLS fingerprint was detected as non-browser traffic. Set up a local reverse proxy using a browser-compatible HTTP client (e.g. Python cloudscraper) and point the provider's baseUrl to it."

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
403 + Cloudflare challenge HTML → classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind
  → isHtmlErrorResponse(403) → "auth_html_403"
  → "Authentication failed" (misleading)

After:
403 + Cloudflare challenge HTML → classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind
  → isCloudflareChallengePage(403) → "cloudflare_challenge"
  → "Cloudflare blocked... TLS fingerprint... reverse proxy" (actionable)

403 + generic HTML → classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind
  → isHtmlErrorResponse(403) → "auth_html_403" (unchanged)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.3 (arm64), Windows 11 Pro
- Runtime/container: Node.js v22.22.2
- Model/provider: openai-codex (chatgpt.com/backend-api)
- Integration/channel: N/A
- Relevant config: Proxy routing traffic through VPN/Quantumult X from mainland China

### Steps

1. Configure `openai-codex` provider with valid OAuth token
2. Route traffic through proxy from mainland China (or any region where Cloudflare triggers JS challenges for Node.js TLS fingerprints)
3. Attempt LLM request via `openai-codex` provider
4. Observe 403 response with Cloudflare challenge HTML

### Expected

Error message explains TLS fingerprint detection and suggests reverse proxy workaround.

### Actual

Before fix: "Authentication failed with an HTML 403 response from the provider."
After fix: "Cloudflare blocked the request with a browser challenge (HTTP 403). Node.js TLS fingerprint was detected as non-browser traffic. Set up a local reverse proxy using a browser-compatible HTTP client (e.g. Python cloudscraper) and point the provider's baseUrl to it."

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

**Test results:**
- 3 new tests added, all passing (39/39 total in test suite)
- Test: "classifies 403 Cloudflare challenge as cloudflare_challenge" ✅
- Test: "classifies non-Cloudflare 403 HTML as auth_html_403 (regression guard)" ✅
- Test: "formats Cloudflare challenge error with TLS fingerprint guidance" ✅

## Human Verification (required)

**Verified scenarios:**
- Cloudflare challenge HTML (with `cf-browser-verification`, `challenges.cloudflare.com`, `_cf_chl_`, `just a moment`, `ray id:`) → `cloudflare_challenge` classification
- Generic 403 HTML (no Cloudflare patterns) → `auth_html_403` classification (regression guard)
- Error message contains "Cloudflare blocked", "TLS fingerprint", "reverse proxy", "cloudscraper"

**Edge cases checked:**
- 403 HTML without Cloudflare patterns still returns `auth_html_403`
- Non-403 status codes with Cloudflare patterns do not trigger `cloudflare_challenge` (status check first)
- HTML responses without `cf-browser-verification` or other markers fall through to generic classification

**What I did NOT verify:**
- Actual Cloudflare challenge page in production (requires mainland China proxy setup)
- Integration with live `openai-codex` provider endpoint

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** False positives — generic HTML pages containing "cloudflare" text might get misclassified as challenges.
  - **Mitigation:** Regex requires multiple CF-specific patterns (`cf-browser-verification`, `challenges.cloudflare.com`, etc.), not just keyword "cloudflare". Pattern is case-insensitive and matches partial strings safely.

- **Risk:** Cloudflare changes challenge page structure, patterns no longer match.
  - **Mitigation:** Regex covers multiple stable markers (div ID, script domain, form field prefix, heading text, Ray ID footer). Unlikely all change simultaneously. If needed, patterns can be updated in future PR.
